content.text:
{The wind blows where it wishes, and you hear its sound, but you do not know where it comes from or where it goes.}{John 3:8}
 {The mathematician does not study pure mathematics because it is useful; he studies it because he delights in it.}{— Henri Poincaré}

The **kernel** and **range** of a linear transformation 
describe what gets "annihilated" and what can be "reached."

content.title: Kernel and Range
content.content:
For a linear transformation $T: V \to W$:

- The **kernel** (or **null space**) is 
 $\ker(T) = \{\vect{v} \in V : T(\vect{v}) = \vect{0}\}$.

- The **range** (or **image**) is 
 $\text{range}(T) = \{T(\vect{v}) : \vect{v} \in V\}$.

content.title: Subspace Properties
content.content: $\ker(T)$ is a subspace of $V$, and $\text{range}(T)$ is a subspace of $W$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Dimension Theorem
content.content:
If $T: V \to W$ is linear and $V$ is finite-dimensional:
\[
\dim(\ker(T)) + \dim(\text{range}(T)) = \dim(V)
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Injective and Surjective
content.content:
A linear transformation $T: V \to W$ is:

- **One-to-one** (injective) if $T(\vect{u}) = T(\vect{v})$ 
 implies $\vect{u} = \vect{v}$.

- **Onto** (surjective) if $\text{range}(T) = W$.

content.title: Characterization
content.content: $T$ is one-to-one if and only if $\ker(T) = \{\vect{0}\}$.
content.label: Theorem

marginNotes.text: Apart from me you can do nothing.
